front: Concept: act only according to maxims that can be willed as a universal law
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: Kingdom of Ends — rational people treat each other as ends in themselves, not means
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: derived from four 'formulas' including the formula of humanity and formula of autonomy
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: deontological ethics — actions judged by the action itself, not consequences; contrasted with consequentialism
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: the only unconditionally good thing is the good will
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: distinguishes acting from duty vs. acting in accordance with duty
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: shopkeeper example — analyzes different motivations for charging fair prices to critique ethical inclinations
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: Christine Korsgaard — major modern Kantian; Sources of Normativity; Creating the Kingdom of Ends; Fellow Creatures on animal rights
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: Schopenhauer criticized Kant's ethics as egoistic in On the Basis of Morality (prize essay for Royal Danish Society); argued compassion is true source of morality
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: Peter Singer attacked Kingdom of Ends as unjust to animals
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: Kantian constructivism — Rawls's view that moral facts come only from procedures for constructing principles of justice
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: sex outside marriage is contemptible because one 'makes himself into a thing'
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: reciprocity thesis — morality and freedom are reciprocal
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: radical evil — innate propensity to adopt maxims prioritizing self-love over moral law; from Religion Within the Bounds of Reason Alone
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Concept: 'fact of reason' — we undeniably know we are free and bound by a moral law
back: The Categorical Imperative & Moral Philosophy (Immanuel Kant)

front: Work: synthetic a priori propositions — truths not merely true by definition; '7 + 5 = 12' as example
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: thing-in-itself (Ding an sich) — unknowable objects unaltered by observation; linked to noumenon; 'two worlds' vs. 'two aspects' interpretations
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: space and time are not empirical concepts but pure intuitions / forms of experience
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: Transcendental Aesthetic — argues space and time are pure intuitions
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: categories of the understanding — 'substance,' 'causality,' etc. structure our experiences; table of judgments (quantity, quality, relation, modality)
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: Copernican Revolution — objects conform to our faculty of intuition, not the reverse
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: Hume woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber' — recounted in preface to Prolegomena
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: A and B editions — citing convention begun with Academy edition published by Dilthey
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: antinomies — theses like 'the world has a limit in space and time' paired with antitheses; two mathematical and two dynamical
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: Transcendental Deduction — proves categories are applicable to experience; Strawson (Bounds of Sense) calls it Kant's most important contribution
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: schematism — chapter on homogeneity of rule and appearance in concepts
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: existence is not a predicate — response to ontological argument for God
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: Eckart Forster's book — systematic reconstruction arguing CPR began philosophy which ended 25 years later
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: Hume's Fork refuted — all judgments split into relations of ideas and matters of fact; Kant argues for a third kind (synthetic a priori)
back: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism (Immanuel Kant)
work: Critique of Pure Reason / Transcendental Idealism

front: Work: four 'moments' of beauty: pleasure and universality among them
Work: beauty has 'universal validity' and is not a 'concept of the object' — judgments of beauty don't presuppose an end ('Zweck')
Work: free beauty vs. adherent beauty (attached to a set of ideas)
Work: four categories of subjective experience: agreeable, beautiful, good, and sublime
Work: the sublime — split into noble, splendid, and terrifying; Analytic of the Sublime section; Lyotard wrote 'Lessons' on it
Work: genius — 'nature giving the rule to art'
Work: sensus communis — community of taste related to two aesthetic concepts
Work: violet color example — 'soft and lovely' to one person, 'dull and faded' to another; form of finality
Work: Hannah Arendt located an 'unwritten political philosophy' in Kant's aesthetics
Work: Theodore Gracyk argued Kant believed all objects can legitimately be claimed to be beautiful
Philosopher: posited four races from a 'lineal root genus'; Galenic four-humor theory adapted into racial hierarchy with Native Americans at bottom
Philosopher: debated definition of race with Georg Forster (scientific racist)
Philosopher: Emmanuel Eze ignited study of Kant's racism in the 1990s
Philosopher: Achille Mbembe's Critique of Black Reason — titled in reference to Kant; theorizes 'Becoming Black of the world'
Philosopher: Gayatri Spivak's Critique of Postcolonial Reason — criticizes 'foreclosure' of 'native informant'
Philosopher: Pauline Kleingeld argues Kant rejected racism in later works
Philosopher: Anthropology from a Pragmatic Point of View — 'Didactic' and 'Characteristic' sections; only white men can reason and have dignity
Philosopher: Adrian Piper obsessively read and chanted Kant in performance Food for the Spirit
Philosopher: gave more lectures on geography than any other subject; sixfold division of geography
Work: three definitive and six preliminary articles for peace; written like a mock treaty
Work: first definitive article: 'the civil constitution of each state shall be Republican'
Work: elimination of assassins and abolition of standing armies among preliminary articles
Work: universal hospitality — limit rights of 'citizens of the world' to hospitality
Work: condemns 'inhospitable behavior of civilised nations' in colonial wars and slave trade
Work: presages democratic peace theory
Work: 'crooked timber of humanity' — 'no straight thing was ever made'; from 'Idea of a Universal History'; Isaiah Berlin titled essay collection after it
Work: Enlightenment = 'man's emergence from his self-incurred immaturity'
Work: motto: Sapere aude! ('Have the courage to know!')
Work: distinguishes public and private use of reason — public = as scholar before 'world of readers'; private = in one's vocation
Work: lost competition to Moses Mendelssohn's less-remembered entry
Work: criticizes 'laziness and cowardice' of men
Philosopher: lying to a murderer at your door is immoral — violates the categorical imperative / unconditionally commanding law
work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

front: Philosopher: Benjamin Constant's critique — if Kant's logic holds, you must tell a murderer your friend's location
work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

front: Philosopher: Kant's reply: 'On a Supposed Right to Tell Lies from Benevolent Motives'
work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

front: Philosopher: Helga Varden added a Nazi officer to the thought experiment
work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

front: Philosopher: Rae Langton's 'Duty and Desolation' — contrasts 'sane' and 'severe' readings
work: The Murderer at the Door (Lying Debate)

front: Philosopher: Marburg school (Hermann Cohen, Ernst Cassirer) — rejected distinction between intuitions and concepts
Philosopher: Southwest school — also rejected intuition/concept distinction
Philosopher: Fischer-Trendelenburg Debate — 'Neglected Alternative' about Kant's theory of space
Philosopher: Hermann Cohen's Kant's Theory of Experience — anti-psychologistic reading; defends against F.A. Lange's criticisms
Philosopher: Windelband — adapted Kant into nomothetic vs. idiographic sciences
Philosopher: Schopenhauer compared reading Kant to 'a cataract operation on a blind person'; also identified thing-in-itself with 'will'
Philosopher: Lange's History of Materialism — major document in neo-Kantianism
Philosopher: Bakhtin drew on Kant in Toward a Philosophy of the Act
Philosopher: Mach and Einstein influenced by Kant's deduction that space and time are forms of experience
Philosopher: John McDowell endorsed Kant's view that raw experiences are endowed with rational content
Work: Prolegomena to Any Future Metaphysics — response to Hume; 'How is metaphysics as a science possible?'; recounts dogmatic slumber
Work: Religion Within the Bounds of Reason Alone — source of radical evil concept
Work: Observations on the Beautiful and the Sublime — early aesthetic work
Work: Physical Monadology — matter is impenetrable due to dynamic equilibrium of attractive and repulsive forces
Work: Opus Postumum — fragmentary; attempted to answer 'How is physics possible?'
Work: Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science — embrace of Newtonian action at a distance led German scientists to reject field theories
Work: 'The End of All Things' — partly satirical philosophy of eschatology
Work: biology cannot be a precise science — 'there will never be a Newton of the blade of grass'
Work: conscience as 'inner court' of justice — examines behavior as if before a judge
Work: What is Orientation in Thinking? — intervention in Mendelssohn-Jacobi feud; 'freedom in thinking signifies subjection of reason to no laws except those it gives itself'
Philosopher: author of the three Critiques (Pure Reason, Practical Reason, Judgment)
Philosopher: never left Königsberg — daily walks set clocks for inhabitants
Philosopher: Prussian philosopher at the Albertina University on Kneiphof (island now named for him)
Philosopher: letter to Lavater condemning 'religion of ingratiation'
